does: This paper introduces MMJ-Bench, a unified evaluation pipeline and benchmark for systematically assessing jailbreak attacks and defense mechanisms targeting Multimodal Large Language Models. The work conducts extensive experiments evaluating multiple attack methods against state-of-the-art MLLMs and measures how various defense strategies impact both their effectiveness at preventing jailbreaks and their effect on normal model utility. By establishing standardized datasets and metrics, MMJ-Bench enables direct comparison of different jailbreak techniques and defenses across consistent evaluation conditions, addressing the fragmentation that previously characterized security research in this domain where each method used different evaluation protocols.
connects: This work builds on the established literature on adversarial attacks and robustness in deep learning, extending prior research on LLM jailbreaking to the multimodal setting. It responds to recognized vulnerabilities in safety alignment mechanisms documented in recent LLM research and acknowledges that MLLMs inherit these risks while introducing additional attack surface through multiple input modalities. The paper connects to prior work on defense mechanisms for language models and safety evaluation frameworks, while recognizing that unified evaluation infrastructure has been lacking for multimodal systems. It synthesizes insights from both adversarial robustness and AI safety alignment literature to address the specific challenge of comprehensive security evaluation.
enables: MMJ-Bench establishes foundational infrastructure for future research on MLLM security by providing standardized benchmarks that facilitate systematic development and comparison of new attack and defense techniques. The work enables researchers to efficiently evaluate novel jailbreak strategies and safety mechanisms against consistent metrics, accelerating progress toward more robust multimodal systems. By clearly documenting the effectiveness and trade-offs of current defenses, it points toward open problems in balancing model capability preservation with security guarantees. The framework supports the development of defense strategies that account for multimodal input vulnerabilities and could inform design principles for fundamentally more robust MLLM architectures.
